title: Fingerprint a Debian or Ubuntu Host
hook: Before package triage, prove what OS family and release you are actually on.
problem: A server is described as Debian or Ubuntu, but you need the exact release fields before interpreting package output.
starting_state: You have shell access to a Debian-family host and need a reliable release snapshot.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads /etc/os-release and prints the package-relevant identity fields.
when_to_use: Use before debugging apt sources, package versions, upgrade paths, or OS support questions.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as proof of the running kernel or CPU architecture.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A compact line such as ubuntu 24.04 noble.

shorts.title: Identify the OS before apt.
shorts.voiceover: Before you debug apt, read the release file. ID, version, and codename tell you which package universe the machine belongs to.
shorts.caption: Start package triage with /etc/os-release.

linkedin.question: Do you check the release codename before reading apt output?
linkedin.cta: Use this as the first line in Debian and Ubuntu inventory notes.

youtube.description: Identify Debian or Ubuntu release fields from /etc/os-release.

title: Compare Kernel and Distro Versions
hook: The distro version and kernel version answer different questions.
problem: You need to know whether a package issue is tied to the OS release, the running kernel, or the CPU architecture.
starting_state: You are collecting a support snapshot from a Debian or Ubuntu system.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command combines kernel, architecture, and distro description in one line.
when_to_use: Use when opening support tickets, comparing fleet nodes, or checking whether the running kernel matches package expectations.
when_not_to_use: Do not treat the kernel version as the installed kernel package list.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A single support-friendly inventory line with kernel, architecture, and distro.

shorts.title: Kernel is not distro.
shorts.voiceover: For package triage, capture the running kernel, CPU architecture, and distro release together.
shorts.caption: One support snapshot: kernel, arch, distro.

linkedin.question: When debugging Linux packages, do you capture kernel and distro separately?
linkedin.cta: Paste this into first-response inventory notes.

youtube.description: Capture Linux kernel, architecture, and Debian or Ubuntu release in one command.

title: List Installed Package Versions
hook: A package inventory beats memory when a server is drifting.
problem: You need a deterministic list of installed Debian packages, versions, and architectures for comparison or incident notes.
starting_state: A Debian or Ubuntu host has packages installed through dpkg and apt.
what_changed: Nothing changes. dpkg-query reads the local package database and prints a sorted inventory.
when_to_use: Use before upgrades, after incidents, during fleet comparison, or when handing a host to another operator.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to discover available updates; use apt list --upgradable for candidate upgrades.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Sorted package, version, and architecture rows.

shorts.title: Export installed versions.
shorts.voiceover: When a machine drifts, do not trust memory. Query dpkg and sort package, version, and architecture.
shorts.caption: Package inventory in one command.

linkedin.question: Do you keep package inventories before risky maintenance windows?
linkedin.cta: Use this for a clean Debian and Ubuntu package snapshot.

youtube.description: Use dpkg-query to list installed Debian or Ubuntu package versions and architectures.

title: See Which Packages Want Updates
hook: Before you upgrade anything, list what would move.
problem: You need to see pending package updates without changing the system.
starting_state: A Debian or Ubuntu machine has apt metadata available and may have pending updates.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command reads apt metadata and prints packages with newer candidate versions.
when_to_use: Use before maintenance windows, security patch review, or explaining why a host is behind.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume this refreshes repository metadata; apt update is a separate state-changing network operation.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Apt rows showing package names, candidate versions, architectures, and installed versions.

shorts.title: List updates first.
shorts.voiceover: Do not start with upgrade. First list the packages that would move and capture the installed versions.
shorts.caption: Read-only apt update triage.

linkedin.question: Do you review the upgradable list before running upgrades?
linkedin.cta: Make this the first check in package maintenance notes.

youtube.description: Inspect pending Debian or Ubuntu package updates without installing them.

title: Check the Installed and Candidate Package Version
hook: apt policy explains where the next version would come from.
problem: A package has an update available, and you need to see the installed version, candidate version, and repository source.
starting_state: You know the package name and want to inspect apt's version decision without installing anything.
what_changed: Nothing changes. apt prints installed and candidate versions plus repository priority lines.
when_to_use: Use when a package version looks wrong, a security pocket matters, or a host is pinned to an older package.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it as proof the service is running; package state and service state are separate.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Installed and candidate package versions with version table entries.

shorts.title: Find apt's candidate.
shorts.voiceover: When a package update looks suspicious, apt policy shows installed version, candidate version, and the repository apt would choose.
shorts.caption: apt policy explains version decisions.

linkedin.question: Do you check apt policy before blaming a repository or mirror?
linkedin.cta: Use this to explain package version decisions.

youtube.description: Inspect installed and candidate package versions with apt policy.

title: Check One Installed Package Cleanly
hook: For one package, dpkg-query gives a clean status line.
problem: You need to confirm whether one package is installed and capture its exact installed version without noisy apt output.
starting_state: You know the package name and need a local installed-state check.
what_changed: Nothing changes. dpkg-query reads the local dpkg database for one package.
when_to_use: Use in runbooks, support tickets, and package audits where one exact installed version matters.
when_not_to_use: Do not use it to find the newest available version; use apt policy for candidate information.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A status and version line such as install ok installed 3.0.13-0ubuntu3.5.

shorts.title: Check one package.
shorts.voiceover: When you only care about one package, query dpkg directly and get the installed status plus exact version.
shorts.caption: One package. One clean status line.

linkedin.question: For one package, do you reach for dpkg-query or apt first?
linkedin.cta: Use this when exact installed state matters.

youtube.description: Check one Debian or Ubuntu package's installed status and version with dpkg-query.

title: Find Which Package Owns a File
hook: That binary came from somewhere. dpkg can tell you where.
problem: You see a file or command on a Debian or Ubuntu host and need to identify the package that installed it.
starting_state: A file exists on disk and may have been installed by a Debian package.
what_changed: Nothing changes. dpkg-query searches the local package ownership database.
when_to_use: Use when auditing binaries, tracing config files, or deciding which package documentation to read.
when_not_to_use: Do not expect it to identify files copied manually outside the package database.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A package-to-path mapping such as nginx: /usr/sbin/nginx.

shorts.title: Find a file's package.
shorts.voiceover: If you find a binary on a Debian system, ask dpkg which package owns that path before guessing.
shorts.caption: Trace a file back to its package.

linkedin.question: Do you trace unfamiliar binaries back to package ownership?
linkedin.cta: Use this before changing files you did not install manually.

youtube.description: Use dpkg-query -S to find which Debian or Ubuntu package owns a file path.

title: Find Broken or Leftover dpkg States
hook: Not every package row is cleanly installed.
problem: A host has package trouble and you need to spot half-configured packages or removed packages with leftover config.
starting_state: The local dpkg database may contain normal installed packages, failed package states, and removed packages with config files left behind.
what_changed: Nothing changes. The command filters dpkg status abbreviations to rows that are not clean ii installed packages.
when_to_use: Use after interrupted installs, failed upgrades, or package repair sessions.
when_not_to_use: Do not delete packages based only on this output; inspect each state and package purpose first.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Rows such as iF app-agent or rc old-kernel-tools that need review.

shorts.title: Find messy dpkg states.
shorts.voiceover: When apt feels broken, inspect dpkg states. Anything not clean ii deserves a closer look before the next repair command.
shorts.caption: Spot half-configured and leftover packages.

linkedin.question: Do you check dpkg status abbreviations after failed package installs?
linkedin.cta: Use this before repair commands so you know what is actually broken.

youtube.description: Find non-clean dpkg package states on Debian and Ubuntu.

experiments.variant_b: When apt feels broken, inspect dpkg.

title: Find the Largest Installed Packages
hook: Disk cleanup starts with evidence, not random package removal.
problem: A Debian or Ubuntu system is low on disk and you need to see which installed packages occupy the most space.
starting_state: The host has limited disk space and package size may be part of the story.
what_changed: Nothing changes. dpkg-query prints installed package sizes from metadata and sorts the largest first.
when_to_use: Use before cleanup conversations, image slimming, or deciding which package families deserve review.
when_not_to_use: Do not remove large packages just because they are large; kernels, runtimes, and databases may be required.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: A size-sorted list of installed packages by KiB.

shorts.title: Find big packages.
shorts.voiceover: Before removing random packages, sort installed package sizes. Cleanup becomes evidence instead of guesswork.
shorts.caption: Largest packages first, no changes made.

linkedin.question: Do package sizes matter in your disk cleanup checklist?
linkedin.cta: Use this read-only view before removal decisions.

youtube.description: Rank installed Debian or Ubuntu packages by installed size.

title: Spot Foreign-Architecture Packages
hook: One unexpected architecture can explain confusing dependency output.
problem: Package dependency output is confusing and you need to see whether the host has non-native architecture packages installed.
starting_state: A Debian or Ubuntu amd64 host may include packages for another architecture such as i386.
what_changed: Nothing changes. dpkg-query prints package architectures and awk filters non-native rows.
when_to_use: Use when debugging multiarch dependency issues, legacy runtime support, or unexpected i386 packages on amd64 servers.
when_not_to_use: Do not assume foreign architecture packages are wrong; some applications require them.
undo: No undo needed because the command is read-only.
expected_output: Foreign-architecture package rows such as i386 zlib1g:i386.

shorts.title: Find foreign arch packages.
shorts.voiceover: On amd64 systems, i386 packages can be legitimate, but they explain dependency surprises. List them before changing apt settings.
shorts.caption: Check multiarch before package repair.

linkedin.question: Have foreign-architecture packages ever explained a dependency puzzle for you?
linkedin.cta: Add this to Debian and Ubuntu package inventory checks.

youtube.description: Find non-native architecture packages on Debian and Ubuntu with dpkg-query.
